---
title: "Annotations"
author: "Ashwin Malshe"
date: "7 October 2025"
format:
  html: 
    theme: vapor
---

Install the following packages from Github. Run this in the console and not inside an R chunk in this notebook!

If you don't have `remotes` installed, run `install.packages("remotes")` in the console before running the following commands.

remotes::install_github("coolbutuseless/svgparser") # Install svgparser from Github
remotes::install_github("wilkelab/gridtext")
remotes::install_github("wilkelab/ggtext")


```{r}
#| label = "setup",
#| include = FALSE

pacman::p_load(tidyverse, showtext, jpeg, png)

library(gridtext)
library(ggtext)

theme_set(theme_minimal())

```


We will use `economics` data set which comes bundled with `ggplot2`. Here is the variable description:

A data frame with 574 rows and 6 variables:

date - Month of data collection
pce - personal consumption expenditures, in billions of dollars
pop - total population, in thousands
psavert - personal savings rate
uempmed - median duration of unemployment, in weeks
unemploy - number of unemployed in thousands


## Image annotation

For annotating plots with images like `geom_text` we have to update the data set with the file path to those images. It's a bit tedious and you should use it only if you really need to. The package for this is `ggimage` and the relevant geom is `geom_image()`. Read more about it here:
https://yulab-smu.top/pkgdocs/ggimage.html


It's far more common to use images to annotate only one point of a plot. For example, you may want to include a logo on the plot either in the background or in one corner. Here is how to do that:

Download UTSA logo (stacked) from here and save it in your project directory:

https://www.utsa.edu/marcomstudio/resources/logos-visual-identity/

Take a note of the file extension. If it is `.png` we will use `png` packages

```{r}
 economics |> 
  mutate(unemploy_per = unemploy / pop) |> 
  ggplot(aes(date, unemploy_per)) +
  annotation_raster(readPNG("UTSA_Logo_Website_UTSA_Logo_Stacked_Center.png"),
                    xmin = -Inf, xmax = Inf, ymin = 0.015, ymax = 0.05, interpolate = T) +
  geom_line(color = "#69be0f", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent)

```


```{r}
 economics |> 
  mutate(unemploy_per = unemploy / pop) |> 
  ggplot(aes(date, unemploy_per)) +
  annotation_raster(readPNG("UTSA_Logo_Website_UTSA_Logo_Stacked_Center.png"),
                    xmin = as.Date("2000-01-01"), xmax = as.Date("2015-01-01"), 
                    ymin = 0.015, ymax = 0.02, interpolate = T) +
  geom_line(color = "#69be0f", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent)

```

If you want to maintain high resolution of the graphics, svg is the best option. Here, we will include a logo in svg format. Note the changes. We can't use `annotation_raster()` anymore as svg is a *vector* graphics file. 

```{r}
 economics |> 
  mutate(unemploy_per = unemploy / pop) |> 
  ggplot(aes(date, unemploy_per)) +
  annotation_custom(svgparser::read_svg('UTSA_Roadrunners_logo.svg'),
                    xmin = as.Date("2005-01-01"), xmax = as.Date("2015-01-01"),
                    ymin = 0.015, ymax = 0.02) +
  geom_line(color = "#69be0f", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent)

```

## Annotations using `ggtext` package

`ggplot2` does not have an easy way to use multiple colors, different fonts, or font faces in the annotations including titles, subtitles, and captions. For professional visualizations, it is common to have such variations. `ggtext` package solves this problem. It allows us to use a subset of HTML, CSS, and markdown with `ggplot2`. 

Check out the Github repo for `ggtext` for more information: https://github.com/wilkelab/ggtext

Create a base plot first.

```{r}
p2 = economics |> 
  mutate(unemploy_per = unemploy / pop) |> 
  ggplot(aes(date, unemploy_per)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Unemployment %")

p2
```

Bold specific words in the title

```{r}
p2 +
  labs(title = "**Rise** and **fall** in the economy are cyclical") +
  theme(plot.title = element_markdown())

```

We can use HTML tags easily. Here, I use "sup" tag which is short for superscript.

```{r}
p2 +
  labs(title = "**Rise** and **fall** in the economy are cyclical",
       subtitle = "This is a finding from UTSA<sup style='font-size:7.0pt'>TM</sup>") +
  theme(plot.title = element_markdown(),
        plot.subtitle = element_markdown())
```

Next, let's make a more complex title using HTML, CSS, and some markdown elements

In HTML, you could use "b" tag for bold and "i" tag for italics font faces. Below I am using "i" tag.

Note that `ggtext` works with only a *subset* of HTML and CSS.



```{r}
p2 +
  labs(title = '<span style="font-size: 14pt;">**The economy is cyclical**</span><br>
<i style="font-size: 11pt;">If it goes <span style="color: green;">**up**</span>, it is almost certain to come <span style="color: red;">**down**</span></i>
') +
  theme(plot.title = element_markdown(lineheight = 1.2))
```

`ggtext` can be used to create text boxes. If the textbox is outside the panel, use `element_textbox()` or `element_textbox_simple()` functions. If the textbox needs to be inside the panel, use `geom_textbox()`.


```{r}
p2 +
  labs(title = '<span style="font-size: 14pt;">**The economy is cyclical**</span><br>
<i style="font-size: 11pt;">If it goes <span style="color: darkgreen;">**up**</span>, it is almost certain to come <span style="color: red;">**down**</span></i>
') +
  theme(plot.title = element_textbox_simple(lineheight = 1.2, fill = "lightsteelblue1",
                                            padding = margin(5.5, 5.5, 5.5, 5.5)))
```

```{r}

text_dt = data.frame(date = as.Date("1990-01-01"), 
                     unemploy_per = 0.0535, 
                     mylabel = '<span style = "color: red">**Reagan recession** </span>')

p2 +
  geom_textbox(data = text_dt, aes(label = mylabel), fill = "lightblue")

```




## Recap: Text annotations

```{r}
p1 = economics |> 
  mutate(year = lubridate::year(date)) |> 
  filter(year >= 2010) |> 
  group_by(year) |> 
  summarize(psavert_mean = mean(psavert, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = as.character(year), y = psavert_mean)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Average Personal savings Rate")

p1
```


```{r}
p1 +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 0.01)(psavert_mean / 100)))
```


```{r}
p1 +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 0.01)(psavert_mean / 100)),
            vjust = 1.5, size = 4, color = "white") # note that these arguments are outside aes()
```

You can set y aesthetic to a constant, which will show the labels at the same height. You may include it inside `aes()` or outside. Here I include it outside the `aes()`.

```{r}
p1 +
  geom_label(aes(label = scales::percent(psavert_mean/100, accuracy = 0.01)), 
            y = 2, size = 4)
```

## Adding annotations for only a few points

`geom_text()` will add text to all the points in the plot. However, often we need to annotate only a few points on the plot. For this purpose, `annotate_*()` functions come in handy.


Use `p2` and annotate 2010 spike in the unemployment

```{r}
p2 +
  annotate(geom = "rect", xmin = as.Date("2008-07-01"), xmax = as.Date("2010-07-01"),
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, fill = "gray30", alpha = 0.5) +
  annotate(geom = "text", label = "Mortgage crisis", 
           x = as.Date("2010-01-01"), y = 0.02,
           color = "red") +
    annotate(geom = "text", label = "Reagan recession", 
           x = as.Date("1990-01-01"), y = 0.051,
           color = "red") 
```

## Practice: Rcessions annotation using Feds data

I downloaded the data as a CSV file from here: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/USREC.

```{r}
rec_dt = read_csv("USREC.csv")
```

The column titled `USREC` is an indicator variable with 1 showing recession very month and 0 showing no recessions. As this time series is really long, going back to 1800s, we must match it with the time series in `economics` dataset.

```{r}
summary(economics$date)
```

```{r}
rec_dt = rec_dt |> 
  rename(date = DATE) |> # here I renamed the variable DATE as date so that it is easier to type
  filter(date >= as.Date("1967-07-01") & date <= as.Date("2015-04-01"))
```

Check if `rec_dt` has the same number of rows as `economics`

```{r}
nrow(rec_dt)
nrow(economics)
```

Recall that to create a rectangle, we need `xmin` and `xmax`. In this case, these dates are beginning  and ending months of a recession. For instance, the recession due to dot.com bubble burst and 9/11 looks like this:

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("Screenshot 2023-10-03 at 10.45.27 PM.png")
```
Thus, the recession started in April 2001 and ended in November 2001. To make a rectangle to show this recession, we will need to use "2001-04-01" as xmin and "2001-11-01" as xmax. So, we need to bring these two values in the same row. This is a challenging task.


```{r}
rec_dt2 = rec_dt |> 
  arrange(date) |> 
  mutate(lag_USREC = lag(USREC),
         lead_USREC = lead(USREC)) |> 
  mutate(recdummy = case_when(
    USREC == 1 & lag_USREC == 0 ~ "rec_start",
    USREC == 1 & lead_USREC == 0 ~ "rec_end",
    TRUE ~ NA)) |> 
  filter(recdummy %in% c("rec_start", "rec_end")) |> 
  select(date, recdummy)
```

```{r}
rec_start_dt = rec_dt2 |> filter(recdummy == "rec_start") |> select(rec_start = date)
rec_end_dt = rec_dt2 |> filter(recdummy == "rec_end") |> select(rec_end = date)

recessions = rec_start_dt |> cbind(rec_end_dt)

print(recessions)
```

Now use this recessions dataset in `geom_rect` to create the final plot.

```{r}
p2 +
  geom_rect(data = recessions, 
            aes(x = NULL, y = NULL, xmin = rec_start, xmax = rec_end, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            fill = "gray30", alpha = 0.5)
```
